section.name: &sect-num;. Getting Started
section.section:
When using BreakTest you will usually follow this process:
To do that, you will run BreakTest in GUI Mode.
Then you can either choose to record the application from a browser, or native application. You can use for that the menu FileTemplates...Recording
Note you can also manually build your plan. Ensure you read this documentation to understand major concepts.
You will also debug it using one of these options:
RunStart no pauses
RunStart
Validate on Thread Group
and View Results Tree renderers or Testers (CSS/JQUERY, JSON, Regexp, XPath).
Ensure you follow best-practices when building your Test Plan.
Once your Test Plan is ready, you can start your Load Test. The first step is to configure the injectors that will run BreakTest, this as for any other Load Testing tool includes:
Correct machine sizing in terms of CPU, memory and network
OS Tuning
Java setup: Ensure you install the latest version of Java supported by BreakTest
Increase the Java Heap size. By default BreakTest starts with a 256 MB heap and can grow up to 2 GB, this might not be enough for your test and depends on your test plan and number of threads you want to run
Once everything is ready, you will use CLI mode (Command-line mode previously called Non-GUI mode) to run it for the Load Test. Don't run load test using GUI mode !
Using CLI mode, you can generate a CSV (or XML) file containing results and have BreakTest generate an HTML report at end of Load Test. BreakTest will by default provide a summary of load test while it's running.
You can also have real-time results during your test using Backend Listener.
Once your Load Test is finished, you can use the HTML report to analyze your load test.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.0.4 Let's start
subsection.p: The easiest way to begin using BreakTest is to first download the latest production release and install it. The release contains all of the files you need to build and run most types of tests, e.g. Web (HTTP/HTTPS), FTP, JDBC, LDAP, Java, JUnit and more.
If you want to perform JDBC testing, then you will, of course, need the appropriate JDBC driver from your vendor. BreakTest does not come with any JDBC drivers.
BreakTest includes the JMS API jar, but does not include a JMS client implementation. If you want to run JMS tests, you will need to download the appropriate jars from the JMS provider.
subsection.note: See the BreakTest Classpath section for details on installing additional jars.
subsection.p: Next, start BreakTest and go through the Building a Test Plan section of the User Guide to familiarize yourself with BreakTest basics (for example, adding and removing elements).

Finally, go through the appropriate section on how to build a specific type of Test Plan. For example, if you are interested in testing a Web application, then see the section Building a Web Test Plan. The other specific Test Plan sections are:
Advanced Web Test Plan
JDBC
FTP
JMS Point-to-Point
JMS Topic
LDAP
LDAP Extended
WebServices (SOAP)
Building Test Plan programmatically

Once you are comfortable with building and running BreakTest Test Plans, you can look into the various configuration elements (timers, listeners, assertions, and others) which give you more control over your Test Plans.

section.name: &sect-num;.1 Requirements
section.p: BreakTest requires that your computing environment meets some minimum requirements.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.1.1 Java Version
subsection.note: BreakTest requires Java 21 or higher. We highly advise you to install the latest patch version of your JDK for security and performance reasons.
subsection.p: Because BreakTest uses only standard Java APIs, please do not file bug reports if your JRE fails to run BreakTest because of JRE implementation issues.
subsection.note: Use a JDK rather than a minimal JRE. HTTPS recording needs the keytool utility from the JDK.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.1.2 Operating Systems
subsection.p: BreakTest is a 100% Java application and should run correctly on any system that has a compliant Java implementation.
BreakTest should run on any operating system with a compliant Java 21 or newer JDK. The project primarily targets current Windows, macOS, and Linux environments.

section.name: &sect-num;.2 Optional
section.p: If you plan on doing BreakTest development, then you will need one or more optional packages listed below.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.2.1 Java Compiler
subsection.p: If you want to build the BreakTest source or develop BreakTest plugins, then you will need a fully compliant JDK 21 or higher.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.2.2 SAX XML Parser
subsection.p: BreakTest comes with Apache's Xerces XML parser. You have the option of telling BreakTest to use a different XML parser. To do so, include the classes for the third-party parser in BreakTest's classpath, and update the jmeter.properties file with the full classname of the parser implementation.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.2.3 Email Support
subsection.p: BreakTest has extensive Email capabilities. It can send email based on test results, and has a POP3(S)/IMAP(S) sampler. It also has an SMTP(S) sampler.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.2.4 SSL Encryption
subsection.p: To test a web server using SSL encryption (HTTPS), BreakTest requires that an implementation of SSL be provided, as is the case with Java 21 and above. If your version of Java does not include SSL support, then it is possible to add an external implementation. Include the necessary encryption packages in BreakTest's classpath. Also, update system.properties to register the SSL Provider.
BreakTest HTTP defaults to protocol level TLS. This can be changed by editing the JMeter property https.default.protocol in jmeter.properties or user.properties.
The BreakTest HTTP samplers are configured to accept all certificates, whether trusted or not, regardless of validity periods, etc. This is to allow the maximum flexibility in testing servers.
If the server requires a client certificate, this can be provided.
There is also the , for greater control of certificates.
subsection.note: The BreakTest proxy server (see below) supports recording HTTPS (SSL)
subsection.p: The SMTP sampler can optionally use a local trust store or trust all certificates.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.2.5 JDBC Driver
subsection.p: You will need to add your database vendor's JDBC driver to the classpath if you want to do JDBC testing. Make sure the file is a jar file, not a zip.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.2.6 JMS client
subsection.p: BreakTest now includes the JMS API from Apache Geronimo, so you just need to add the appropriate JMS Client implementation jar(s) from the JMS provider. Please refer to their documentation for details. Consult your JMS provider documentation for the required client jars.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.2.7 Libraries for ActiveMQ JMS
subsection.p: You will need to add the jar activemq-all-X.X.X.jar to your classpath, e.g. by storing it in the lib/ directory.
See ActiveMQ initial configuration page for details.

section.note: See the BreakTest Classpath section for more details on installing additional jars.

section.name: &sect-num;.3 Installation
section.p: We recommend that most users run the latest BreakTest release.

To install a release build, simply unzip the zip/tar file into the directory where you want BreakTest to be installed. Provided that you have a JRE/JDK correctly installed and the JAVA_HOME environment variable set, there is nothing more for you to do.

The installation directory structure should look something like this (where X.Y is version number):
breaktest-X.Y breaktest-X.Y/bin breaktest-X.Y/docs breaktest-X.Y/extras breaktest-X.Y/lib/ breaktest-X.Y/lib/ext breaktest-X.Y/lib/junit breaktest-X.Y/licenses breaktest-X.Y/docs
You can rename the parent directory (i.e. breaktest-X.Y) if you want, but do not change any of the sub-directory names.

section.name: &sect-num;.4 Running BreakTest
section.p: To run BreakTest, run the breaktest.bat (for Windows) or breaktest (for Unix) file. These files are found in the bin directory. After a short time, the BreakTest GUI should appear. GUI mode should only be used for creating the test script, CLI mode (NON GUI) must be used for load testing
There are some additional scripts in the bin directory that you may find useful. Windows script files (the .CMD files require Win2K or later):

dl.dd: run BreakTest (in GUI mode by default)
run BreakTest without the windows shell console (in GUI mode by default)
drop a JMX file on this to run a CLI mode test
drop a JMX file on this to load it in GUI mode
runs the BreakTest Mirror Server in CLI mode
pause a running CLI mode instance
resume a paused CLI mode instance
stop a CLI mode instance gracefully
stop a CLI mode instance abruptly

section.note: The special name LAST can be used with breaktest-n.cmd and breaktest-t.cmd and means the last test plan that was run interactively.
section.p: There are a few environment variables, that can be used to customize the JVM settings for BreakTest. An easy way to set those is by creating a file named setenv.bat in the bin directory. Such a file could look like:
section.source:
rem This is the content of bin\setenv.bat, rem it will be called by bin\breaktest.bat
set JVM_ARGS=-Xms1024m -Xmx1024m -Dpropname=value
section.p: The JVM_ARGS can be used to override JVM settings in the breaktest.bat script and will get set when starting BreakTest, e.g.:
The following environment variables can be defined:

dl.dd: JVM options to influence usage of direct draw, e.g. -Dsun.java2d.ddscale=true. Default is empty.
JVM garbage collector options. Defaults to -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=250 -XX:G1ReservePercent=20
JVM memory settings used when starting BreakTest. Defaults to -Xms256m -Xmx2g -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=256m
BreakTest bin directory (must end in \). The legacy variable name is retained for compatibility.
If set, only JVM_ARGS and JMETER_OPTS are used. All other options like HEAP and GC_ALGO are ignored. The legacy variable name is retained for compatibility.
BreakTest installation directory. The legacy variable name is retained for compatibility and is guessed from the location of breaktest.bat.
Java runtime options to specify used language. Defaults to: -Duser.language="en" -Duser.region="EN"
Name of the java executable, like java.exe (default) or javaw.exe
Java options to be used when starting BreakTest. These will be added last to the java command. Default is empty

section.p: Un*x script files; should work on most Linux/Unix systems:

dl.dd: run BreakTest (in GUI mode by default). Defines some JVM settings which may not work for all JVMs.
very basic BreakTest script (You may need to adapt JVM options like memory settings).
runs the BreakTest Mirror Server in CLI mode
starts the BreakTest MCP server used by AI-assisted scripting tools
runs the BreakTest local agent tool used by AI-assisted scripting workflows
pause a running CLI mode instance
resume a paused CLI mode instance
stop a CLI mode instance gracefully
stop a CLI mode instance abruptly

section.p: It may be necessary to set a few environment variables to configure the JVM used by BreakTest. Those variables can be either set directly in the shell starting the breaktest script. For example setting the variable JVM_ARGS will override most pre-defined settings, for example
will override the HEAP settings in the script.

To set those variables permanently, you can place them in a file called setenv.sh in the bin directory. This file will be sourced when running BreakTest by calling the breaktest script. An example for bin/setenv.sh could look like:
section.source:
# This is the file bin/setenv.sh, # it will be sourced in by bin/breaktest
# Use a bigger heap, but a smaller metaspace, than the default export HEAP="-Xms512m -Xmx4G -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=192m"
# Try to guess the locale from the OS. The space as value is on purpose! export JMETER_LANGUAGE=" "
section.p: The following environment variables can be defined:

dl.dd: Java runtime options to specify JVM garbage collection algorithm. Defaults to -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=250 -XX:G1ReservePercent=20
Java runtime options for memory management used when BreakTest is started. Defaults to -Xms256m -Xmx2g -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=256m
Must point at your Java Development Kit installation. Required to run the with the "debug" argument. On some OSes BreakTest will try its best to guess the location of the JVM.
If set, only JVM_ARGS and JMETER_OPTS are used. All other options like HEAP and GC_ALGO are ignored. The legacy variable name is retained for compatibility.
May point to your BreakTest install dir. If empty it will be set relative to the breaktest script. The legacy variable name is retained for compatibility.
Java runtime options to specify used language. Defaults to -Duser.language=en -Duser.region=EN
Java runtime options used when BreakTest is started. The legacy variable name is retained for compatibility.

Must point at your Java Runtime installation. Defaults to JAVA_HOME if empty. If JRE_HOME and JAVA_HOME are both empty, BreakTest will try to guess JAVA_HOME. If JRE_HOME and JAVA_HOME are both set, JAVA_HOME is used.
Java options to be used when starting BreakTest. These will be added before JMETER_OPTS and after the other JVM options. Default is empty

subsection.name: &sect-num;.4.1 BreakTest Classpath
subsection.p: BreakTest automatically finds classes from jars in the following directories:

dl.dd: used for utility jars
used for BreakTest/JMeter-compatible components and plugins

subsection.p: If you have developed new BreakTest or JMeter-compatible components, then you should jar them and copy the jar into BreakTest's lib/ext directory. BreakTest will automatically find compatible components in any jars found here. Do not use lib/ext for utility jars or dependency jars used by the plugins; it is only intended for BreakTest/JMeter-compatible components and plugins.
If you don't want to put plugin jars in the lib/ext directory, then define the property search_paths in jmeter.properties.
Utility and dependency jars (libraries etc) can be placed in the lib directory.
If you don't want to put such jars in the lib directory, then define the property user.classpath or plugin_dependency_paths in jmeter.properties. See below for an explanation of the differences.

Other jars (such as JDBC, JMS implementations and any other support libraries needed by the BreakTest code) should be placed in the lib directory - not the lib/ext directory, or added to user.classpath.
subsection.note: BreakTest will only find .jar files, not .zip.
subsection.p: You can also install utility Jar files in $JAVA_HOME/jre/lib/ext, or you can set the property user.classpath in jmeter.properties

Note that setting the CLASSPATH environment variable will have no effect. This is because BreakTest is started with "java -jar", and the java command silently ignores the CLASSPATH variable, and the -classpath/-cp options when -jar is used.
subsection.note: This occurs with all Java programs, not just BreakTest.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.4.2 Create Test Plan from Template
subsection.p: You have the ability to create a new Test Plan from existing template.

To do so you use the menu
File Templates… or Templates icon: Templates icon item
A popup appears, you can then choose a template among the list: Templates popup
Some templates may need parameters input from the user. For theses ones, after a click on the create button, a new window will appear as follow : Parameters window
When you are done with parameters, click on the Validate button and the template will be created.
A documentation for each template explains what to do once test plan is created from template.
subsection.note: You can create your own templates following documentation here

section.subsection:
If you are testing from behind a firewall/proxy server, you may need to provide BreakTest with the firewall/proxy server hostname and port number. To do so, run the breaktest[.bat] file from a command line with the following parameters:
-E[proxy scheme to use - optional - for non-http] -H[proxy server hostname or ip address] -P[proxy server port] -N[nonproxy hosts] (e.g. *.apache.org|localhost) -u[username for proxy authentication - if required] -a[password for proxy authentication - if required]
Example: breaktest -E https -H my.proxy.server -P 8000 -u username -a password -N localhost
You can also use --proxyScheme, --proxyHost, --proxyPort, --username, and --password as parameter names
Parameters provided on a command-line may be visible to other users on the system.
If the proxy scheme is provided, then BreakTest sets the following System properties:
http.proxyScheme
If the proxy host and port are provided, then BreakTest sets the following System properties:
http.proxyHost
http.proxyPort
https.proxyHost
https.proxyPort
The user and password used for a proxy can be given by the System properties http.proxyUser and http.proxyUser. They will get overridden by the above arguments or values set in the HTTP Samplers.
If a nonproxy host list is provided, then BreakTest sets the following System properties:
http.nonProxyHosts
https.nonProxyHosts
So if you don't wish to set both http and https proxies, you can define the relevant properties in system.properties instead of using the command-line parameters.
Proxy Settings can also be defined in a Test Plan, using either the configuration or the sampler elements.
BreakTest also has its own in-built Proxy Server, the HTTP(S) Test Script Recorder. This is only used for recording HTTP or HTTPS browser sessions. This is not to be confused with the proxy settings described above, which are used when BreakTest makes HTTP or HTTPS requests itself.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.4.4 CLI Mode (Command Line mode was called NON GUI mode)
subsection.p: For load testing, you must run BreakTest in this mode (Without the GUI) to get the optimal results from it. To do so, use the following command options:

dl.dd: This specifies BreakTest is to run in cli mode
[name of JMX file that contains the Test Plan].
[name of JTL file to log sample results to].
[name of BreakTest run log file].
[path to CSV file] generate report dashboard only
generate report dashboard after load test
output folder where to generate the report dashboard after load test. Folder must not exist or be empty

subsection.p: The script also lets you specify the optional firewall/proxy server information:

dl.dd: [proxy server hostname or ip address]
[proxy server port]

subsection.p: If the property jmeterengine.stopfail.system.exit is set to true (default is false), then BreakTest will invoke System.exit(1) if it cannot stop all threads. Normally this is not necessary.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.4.6 Overriding Properties Via The Command Line
subsection.p: Java system properties and BreakTest/JMeter compatibility properties can be overridden directly on the command line (instead of modifying jmeter.properties). To do so, use the following options:

dl.dd: defines a java system property value.
defines a local BreakTest/JMeter compatibility property.
overrides a logging setting, setting a particular category to the given priority level.

subsection.p: The -L flag can also be used without the category name to set the root logging level.
Examples:
subsection.note: The command line properties are processed early in startup, but after the logging system has been set up.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.4.7 Logging and error messages
subsection.note: Since 3.2, logging is not configured through properties file(s) such as jmeter.properties any more, but it is configured through a Apache Log4j 2 configuration file (log4j2.xml in the directory from which BreakTest was launched, by default) instead. Also, every component including BreakTest and plugins MUST use SLF4J library to leave logs since 3.2.
subsection.p: Here is an example log4j2.xml file which defines two log appenders and loggers for each category.
subsection.source:
<Configuration status="WARN" packages="org.apache.jmeter.gui.logging">
<Appenders>
<!-- The main log file appender to jmeter.log in the directory from which BreakTest was launched, by default. --> <File name="jmeter-log" fileName="${sys:jmeter.logfile:-jmeter.log}" append="false"> <PatternLayout> <pattern>%d %p %c{1.}: %m%n</pattern> </PatternLayout> </File>
<!-- Log appender for GUI Log Viewer. See below. --> <GuiLogEvent name="gui-log-event"> <PatternLayout> <pattern>%d %p %c{1.}: %m%n</pattern> </PatternLayout> </GuiLogEvent>
</Appenders>
<Loggers>
<!-- Root logger --> <Root level="info"> <AppenderRef ref="jmeter-log" /> <AppenderRef ref="gui-log-event" /> </Root>
<!-- SNIP -->
<!-- # Apache HttpClient logging examples --> <!-- # Enable header wire + context logging - Best for Debugging --> <!-- <Logger name="org.apache.hc.client5.http" level="debug" /> <Logger name="org.apache.hc.client5.http.wire" level="error" /> -->
<!-- SNIP -->
</Loggers>
</Configuration>
subsection.p: So, if you want to change the log level for org.apache.hc.client5.http category to debug level for instance, you can simply add (or uncomment) the following logger element in log4j2.xml file before launching BreakTest.
For more detail on how to configure log4j2.xml file, please see Apache Log4j 2 Configuration page.
Log level for specific categories or root logger can be overridden directly on the command line (instead of modifying log4j2.xml) as well. To do so, use the following options:

dl.dd: Overrides a logging setting, setting a particular category to the given priority level. Since 3.2, it is recommended to use a full category name (e.g, org.apache.jmeter or com.example.foo), but if the category name starts with either jmeter or jorphan, org.apache. will be prepended internally to the category name input to construct a full category name (i.e, org.apache.jmeter or org.apache.jorphan) for backward compatibility.

subsection.p: Examples:
Differences in Logging : Old vs New Practices:

As BreakTest uses SLF4J as logging API and Apache Log4j 2 as a logging framework since 3.2, not every log level used before 3.2 can match exactly with one of the new available log levels provided by SLF4J/Log4j2. Therefore, please keep the following differences and new suggested practices in mind if you need to migrate any existing logging configurations and logging code.

tr.td: Logger reference through the removed LogKit compatibility API: Legacy LoggingManager / LogKit Logger references
Log Levels in Configuration or Command Line Arguments

Old Log Levels:
DEBUG
INFO
WARN
ERROR
FATAL_ERROR
NONE

Mapping to New Levels through SLF4J/Log4j2:
DEBUG
INFO
WARN
ERROR
ERROR
OFF
Since FATAL_ERROR is not supported by SLF4J API, it is treated as ERROR instead for existing code not to break. There is also the FATAL log level option.
TRACE level, which is less specific than DEBUG, is supported additionally since 3.2. Look up SLF4J or Apache Log4J 2 documentations for details.

subsection.note: BreakTest does not generally use pop-up dialog boxes for errors, as these would interfere with running tests. Nor does it report any error for a mis-spelt variable or function; instead the reference is just used as is. See Functions and Variables for more information.
subsection.p: If BreakTest detects an error during a test, a message will be written to the log file. The log file name is defined in the log4j2.xml file (or using the -j option, see below). It defaults to jmeter.log, and will be found in the directory from which BreakTest was launched.
The menu OptionsLog Viewer displays the log file in a bottom pane on main BreakTest window.
In the GUI mode, the number of error/fatal messages logged in the log file is displayed at top-right.

The command-line option -j jmeterlogfile allow to process after the initial properties file is read, and before any further properties are processed. It therefore allows the default of jmeter.log to be overridden. The BreakTest scripts that take a test plan name as a parameter (e.g. breaktest-n.cmd) have been updated to define the log file using the test plan name, e.g. for the test plan Test27.jmx the log file is set to Test27.log.

When running on Windows, the file may appear as just jmeter unless you have set Windows to show file extensions. [Which you should do anyway, to make it easier to detect viruses and other nasties that pretend to be text files …]
As well as recording errors, the jmeter.log compatibility log file records some information about the test run. For example:
subsection.source: 2026-07-03 12:19:20,314 INFO o.a.j.JMeter: Version 2026.07.03 2026-07-03 12:19:45,314 INFO o.a.j.g.a.Load: Loading file: c:\mytestfiles\checkout.jmx 2026-07-03 12:19:52,328 INFO o.a.j.e.StandardJMeterEngine: Running the test! 2026-07-03 12:19:52,384 INFO o.a.j.e.StandardJMeterEngine: Starting 1 threads for group Checkout. Ramp up = 1. 2026-07-03 12:19:52,485 INFO o.a.j.e.StandardJMeterEngine: Continue on error 2026-07-03 12:19:52,589 INFO o.a.j.t.JMeterThread: Thread Checkout-1 started 2026-07-03 12:19:52,590 INFO o.a.j.t.JMeterThread: Thread Checkout-1 is done 2026-07-03 12:19:52,691 INFO o.a.j.e.StandardJMeterEngine: Test has ended
subsection.p: The log file can be helpful in determining the cause of an error, as BreakTest does not interrupt a test to display an error dialogue.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.4.8 Full list of command-line options
subsection.p: Invoking BreakTest as "breaktest -?" will print a list of all the command-line options. These are shown below.
subsection.source: --? print command line options and exit -h, --help print usage information and exit -v, --version print the version information and exit -p, --propfile <argument> the BreakTest property file to use; the file is commonly named jmeter.properties for compatibility -q, --addprop <argument> additional BreakTest/JMeter compatibility property file(s) -t, --testfile <argument> the JMX test plan file to run -l, --logfile <argument> the file to log samples to -i, --jmeterlogconf <argument> logging configuration file (log4j2.xml) -j, --jmeterlogfile <argument> BreakTest run log file; the default name is jmeter.log for compatibility -n, --nongui run BreakTest in nongui mode -s, --server removed: RMI Remote Server mode is not supported in BreakTest -H, --proxyHost <argument> Set a proxy server for BreakTest to use -P, --proxyPort <argument> Set proxy server port for BreakTest to use -N, --nonProxyHosts <argument> Set nonproxy host list (e.g. *.apache.org|localhost) -u, --username <argument> Set username for proxy server that BreakTest is to use -a, --password <argument> Set password for proxy server that BreakTest is to use -J, --jmeterproperty <argument>=<value> Define additional BreakTest/JMeter compatibility properties -D, --systemproperty <argument>=<value> Define additional system properties -S, --systemPropertyFile <argument> additional system property file(s) -f, --forceDeleteResultFile force delete existing results files and web report folder if present before starting the test -L, --loglevel <argument>=<value> [category=]level e.g. jorphan=INFO, jmeter.util=DEBUG or com.example.foo=WARN -d, --homedir <argument> the BreakTest home directory to use -g, --reportonly <argument> generate report dashboard only, from a test results file -e, --reportatendofloadtests generate report dashboard after load test -o, --reportoutputfolder <argument> output folder for report dashboard
subsection.p: Note: the BreakTest log file name is formatted as a SimpleDateFormat (applied to the current date) if it contains paired single-quotes, .e.g. 'jmeter_'yyyyMMddHHmmss'.log'
If the special name LAST is used for the -t, -j or -l flags, then BreakTest takes that to mean the last test plan that was run in interactive mode.

subsection.name: &sect-num;.4.9 CLI mode shutdown
subsection.p: Prior to version 2.5.1, BreakTest inherited JMeter behavior that invoked System.exit() when a CLI mode test completed. This caused problems for applications that invoke BreakTest directly, so BreakTest no longer invokes System.exit() for a normal test completion. [Some fatal errors may still invoke System.exit()] BreakTest will exit all the non-daemon threads it starts, but it is possible that some non-daemon threads may still remain; these will prevent the JVM from exiting. To detect this situation, BreakTest starts a new daemon thread just before it exits. This daemon thread waits a short while; if it returns from the wait, then clearly the JVM has not been able to exit, and the thread prints a message to say why.

The property jmeter.exit.check.pause can be used to configure the delay before printing non-daemon threads. If set to 0 (default value), then BreakTest does not print non-terminated threads at the end of the test.

section.name: &sect-num;.5 Configuring BreakTest
section.p: If you wish to modify the properties with which BreakTest runs you need to either modify the user.properties in the /bin directory or create your own copy of the jmeter.properties and specify it in the command line.
section.note: Note: You can define additional BreakTest/JMeter compatibility properties in the file defined by the BreakTest compatibility property user.properties which has the default value user.properties. The file will be automatically loaded if it is found in the current directory or if it is found in the BreakTest bin directory. Similarly, system.properties is used to update system properties.

properties.property: You can specify the class for your SSL implementation if you don't want to use the built-in Java implementation.
You can specify an implementation as your XML parser.

A list of components you do not want to see in BreakTest menus. As BreakTest has more components added, you may wish to customize BreakTest to show only those components you are interested in. You may list their classname or their class label (the string that appears in BreakTest's UI) here, and they will no longer appear in the menus.

List of paths (separated by ;) that BreakTest will search for compatible plugin classes, for example additional samplers. A path item can either be a jar file or a directory. Any jar file in such a directory will be automatically included in search_paths, jar files in sub directories are ignored. The given value is in addition to any jars found in the lib/ext directory.

List of paths that BreakTest will search for utility and plugin dependency classes. Use your platform path separator to separate multiple paths. A path item can either be a jar file or a directory. Any jar file in such a directory will be automatically included in user.classpath, jar files in sub directories are ignored. The given value is in addition to any jars found in the lib directory. All entries will be added to the class path of the system class loader and also to the path of the BreakTest internal loader.

List of paths (separated by ;) that BreakTest will search for utility and plugin dependency classes. A path item can either be a jar file or a directory. Any jar file in such a directory will be automatically included in plugin_dependency_paths, jar files in sub directories are ignored. The given value is in addition to any jars found in the lib directory or given by the user.classpath property. All entries will be added to the path of the BreakTest internal loader only. For plugin dependencies using plugin_dependency_paths should be preferred over user.classpath.
Name of file containing additional BreakTest/JMeter compatibility properties. These are added after the initial property file, but before the -q and -J options are processed.
Name of file containing additional system properties. These are added before the -S and -D options are processed.

section.p:
The command line options and properties files are processed in the following order:
-p propfile
jmeter.properties (or the file from the -p option) is then loaded
-j logfile
Logging is initialised
user.properties is loaded
system.properties is loaded
all other command-line options are processed

p.b: See also the comments in the jmeter.properties, user.properties and system.properties files for further information on other settings you can change.
